expanded_description: A clear introduction to plant tissue systems for secondary school biology. This covers the three main plant tissue systems—dermal (protection), ground (metabolism, support, storage), and vascular (transport)—and how they are organized differently in roots, stems, and leaves to help plants grow, survive, and adapt to their environment.

core_concept.principle: Plants have three tissue systems that work together: dermal tissue (outer protection), ground tissue (metabolism, support, storage), and vascular tissue (transport). These systems are continuous throughout the plant but organized differently in roots, stems, and leaves to suit each organ's function.
core_concept.explanation:
If you look at a plant, you will see that it is not just a collection of cells. The cells are organized into tissues, and the tissues are organized into three systems that span the entire plant. These systems work together to keep the plant alive.

The dermal tissue system is the plant's skin. In young plants, it is a single layer of cells called the epidermis. The epidermis secretes a waxy coating (cuticle) that prevents water loss. It also has tiny pores (stomata) that open and close to let carbon dioxide in for photosynthesis and oxygen out. In woody plants, the epidermis is replaced by periderm (bark), which provides thicker protection.

The ground tissue system is the bulk of the plant. It does most of the work. In leaves, ground tissue (mesophyll) performs photosynthesis. In stems, it provides support and stores water and nutrients. In roots, it stores starch and helps transport water to the vascular tissue. Ground tissue is made of three cell types: parenchyma (thin walls, metabolism), collenchyma (uneven walls, flexible support), and sclerenchyma (thick lignified walls, rigid support).

The vascular tissue system is the plant's plumbing. It has two types of tubes. Xylem carries water and dissolved minerals from the roots up to the leaves. Phloem carries sugars (made in the leaves) to the rest of the plant. Xylem cells are dead at maturity—they are hollow tubes that water flows through. Phloem cells are alive—they need energy to move sugar.

These three systems are organized differently in different parts of the plant. In roots, the dermal tissue has root hairs (for absorbing water), the ground tissue is mostly storage parenchyma, and the vascular tissue is in the center (a central cylinder called the stele). In stems, the dermal tissue is the epidermis (or bark), the ground tissue is divided into cortex (outer) and pith (center), and the vascular tissue is arranged in bundles. In leaves, the dermal tissue has stomata for gas exchange, the ground tissue is mesophyll (for photosynthesis), and the vascular tissue forms veins.

Understanding plant tissue systems helps us understand how plants grow, how they transport water and sugar, and how we can improve crops for food, fiber, and fuel.

image.alt: A diagram showing the three tissue systems—dermal (outer layer), ground (bulk of plant), vascular (transport tubes)—and how they are arranged in a root, stem, and leaf
image.caption: Plant tissue systems: Dermal (protection), Ground (metabolism, support, storage), Vascular (transport). Each system is organized differently in roots, stems, and leaves to suit the organ's function.

sections.introduction:
Plants are different from animals in an important way: they cannot move. An animal can walk to food, run from danger, or swim to a new home. A plant must get everything it needs—water, minerals, sunlight, carbon dioxide—from where it is rooted. To do this, plants need an efficient way to absorb resources, transport them throughout the body, and use them where they are needed.

This is why plants have tissue systems. A tissue is a group of cells that work together to perform a specific function. In plants, these tissues are organized into three systems that span the whole plant: dermal (the skin), ground (the bulk), and vascular (the plumbing).

The dermal system is the outer layer. It protects the plant from water loss, disease, and injury. In leaves, it has tiny openings (stomata) that let carbon dioxide in and oxygen out. In roots, it has root hairs that absorb water and minerals from the soil.

The ground system is everything between the dermal and vascular tissues. It does most of the plant's work. In leaves, ground tissue (mesophyll) performs photosynthesis. In stems, it provides support and stores water and food. In roots, it stores starch.

The vascular system is the transport network. It has two types of tubes. Xylem carries water and minerals from the roots up to the leaves. Phloem carries sugar from the leaves down to the rest of the plant. These tubes run through the roots, stems, and leaves, connecting all parts of the plant.

These three systems are not the same everywhere. A root needs to absorb water, so it has root hairs and a central vascular cylinder. A stem needs to support the plant against gravity, so it has strong fibers and a ring of vascular bundles. A leaf needs to capture light, so it is thin and flat with lots of chloroplasts. Each organ has the same three tissue systems, but they are arranged differently to suit the organ's job.

Understanding plant tissue systems helps us understand how plants work. It also helps us grow better crops—we can breed plants with stronger stems, better water transport, or more efficient photosynthesis. From the food we eat to the wood we build with, plant tissues are the foundation of agriculture and forestry.

introduction_image.alt: A diagram showing the three tissue systems in a whole plant: dermal (outer layer), ground (filling), vascular (tubes) from roots to leaves
introduction_image.caption: Plant tissue systems are continuous throughout the plant. Dermal tissue covers the outside, ground tissue fills the interior, and vascular tissue connects roots to leaves, transporting water and sugar.

key_concept.definition: Plants have three tissue systems: dermal (protection), ground (metabolism, support, storage), and vascular (transport). These systems are organized differently in roots, stems, and leaves to perform organ-specific functions.
key_concept.context:
The dermal system is the outer covering. In young plants, it is the epidermis (a single layer of cells) with a waxy cuticle to prevent water loss. In woody plants, the epidermis is replaced by bark (periderm). The epidermis also has stomata (pores that open and close for gas exchange) and trichomes (hairs that protect against insects and reduce water loss).

The ground system makes up most of the plant body. It is made of three cell types. Parenchyma cells have thin walls and do most of the metabolic work (photosynthesis, storage). Collenchyma cells have unevenly thickened walls and provide flexible support in growing stems. Sclerenchyma cells have thick, lignified walls and provide rigid support in mature parts.

The vascular system has two types of conducting tissue. Xylem carries water and minerals upward from roots to leaves. It is made of dead cells with hollow interiors. Phloem carries sugar from leaves to other parts of the plant. It is made of living cells that need energy to work.

The arrangement of these tissues changes depending on the organ. Roots have root hairs (for absorption), a cortex (ground tissue for storage), and a central stele (vascular tissue). Stems have epidermis, cortex, vascular bundles (in a ring or scattered), and pith. Leaves have epidermis with stomata, mesophyll (ground tissue for photosynthesis), and veins (vascular tissue).

image.alt: Cross-sections of a root, stem, and leaf showing the arrangement of dermal, ground, and vascular tissues
image.caption: Tissue organization: Root (epidermis with root hairs, cortex, central stele), Stem (epidermis, cortex, vascular bundles, pith), Leaf (epidermis with stomata, mesophyll, veins). Each organ has the same three systems, arranged differently.

sections.key_concept: The three tissue systems—dermal, ground, and vascular—work together as an integrated whole. Dermal protects and regulates exchange; ground does the work; vascular transports resources. Their arrangement in each organ reflects that organ's function.

classifications.value: Understanding dermal tissue explains how plants protect themselves from water loss, disease, and herbivores, while still allowing gas exchange for photosynthesis.
characteristics: Epidermis: Single layer of cells covering young plants and non-woody parts. Secretes a waxy cuticle that prevents water loss.
Cuticle: Waxy layer on the outside of the epidermis. Thicker in dry environments, thinner in wet environments.
Stomata: Pores in the epidermis, each flanked by two guard cells. Open to take in CO₂ and release O₂, close to prevent water loss.
Trichomes: Hair-like outgrowths from the epidermis. Protect against insects, reflect sunlight, reduce water loss.
Root hairs: Tubular extensions of root epidermal cells. Increase surface area for water and mineral absorption.
Periderm: Bark that replaces epidermis in woody stems and roots. Made of cork cells (dead, waterproof) with lenticels for gas exchange.
classifications.behavior:
The dermal system has a tough job: it must protect the plant from the outside world while still allowing essential exchanges. The cuticle is a waxy barrier that stops water from evaporating out of the plant. But if it stopped everything, the plant could not take in carbon dioxide for photosynthesis or release oxygen. This is where stomata come in.

Stomata are tiny pores, usually on the underside of leaves. Each pore is surrounded by two guard cells. When the guard cells take up water, they swell and bend open, creating a hole. When they lose water, they relax and close the hole. The plant controls this opening and closing based on light (open in light), carbon dioxide levels (open when CO₂ is low), and water availability (close when water is scarce). This lets the plant balance its need for CO₂ with its need to conserve water.

In dry environments, plants have thicker cuticles, fewer stomata (or stomata sunk into pits), and dense trichomes that trap moisture and reflect sunlight. In wet environments, cuticles are thin, stomata are abundant, and trichomes are fewer.

Root hairs are another dermal adaptation. A single root can have millions of root hairs, each a thin extension of an epidermal cell. Root hairs dramatically increase the surface area for water and mineral absorption. A plant without root hairs would have a hard time getting enough water, even in wet soil.

As plants grow and become woody, the epidermis is replaced by periderm (bark). Bark is made of dead cells with waterproof walls (cork). It protects the tree from fire, insects, and temperature extremes. But bark also needs to let gases through, so it has lenticels—small, corky bumps that allow air to reach the living cells underneath.

examples.process: Stomata: How Plants Breathe and Conserve Water
examples.explanation:
Stomata are the plant's compromise between getting carbon dioxide and not losing too much water. Each stoma is a pore surrounded by two guard cells. Here is how they work:

When the sun is shining, the guard cells take up potassium ions. Water follows by osmosis, and the guard cells swell. Because their walls are thicker on the inside, they bend outward, opening the pore. Carbon dioxide enters, oxygen exits, and photosynthesis happens.

When the plant is short of water (drought), a hormone called ABA tells the guard cells to let potassium out. Water follows, the guard cells relax, and the pore closes. This prevents water from escaping through the leaves.

Stomata also respond to carbon dioxide. If CO₂ levels inside the leaf are low (because the plant is photosynthesizing quickly), the stomata open wider to let more in. If CO₂ levels are high, they close.

In dry environments, plants have adaptations to reduce water loss through stomata. Some have stomata sunk into pits (so they are sheltered from wind). Some have stomata only on the underside of leaves (where it is cooler and less windy). Some have dense hairs that trap moisture. Cacti open their stomata at night (when it is cooler) and store CO₂ for daytime photosynthesis. This is called CAM photosynthesis.

image.alt: Stomata opening and closing: guard cells take up water and swell to open, lose water and relax to close
image.caption: Stomata: Guard cells take up K⁺ and water → swell → pore opens (CO₂ in, O₂ out). Lose water → relax → pore closes (prevents water loss). Controlled by light, CO₂, and water availability.

examples.process: Root Hairs: Absorbing Water and Minerals
examples.explanation:
Root hairs are tiny extensions of root epidermal cells. They are so small that you need a microscope to see them, but they are critical for the plant's survival.

A single root hair is a thin tube (about 10 micrometers wide) that grows out from a root epidermal cell. It lives for only a few days, but new root hairs are constantly forming. A single plant can have billions of root hairs.

What do root hairs do? They increase the surface area of the root. A root without root hairs has a certain surface area. With root hairs, the surface area can be 10 to 20 times larger. This allows the plant to absorb much more water and minerals from the soil.

Root hairs also secrete acids and enzymes that help dissolve minerals in the soil. They can release protons (H⁺) to exchange for nutrients like potassium (K⁺) and nitrate (NO₃⁻). They can also form partnerships with fungi (mycorrhizae) that help them absorb even more nutrients.

When a plant is transplanted, the root hairs are often damaged. That is why transplanted plants need extra water—they cannot absorb as much until new root hairs grow. This is also why it is best to transplant in cool, cloudy weather, when water loss from leaves is less.

image.alt: Root tip with root hairs extending into soil, increasing surface area for absorption
image.caption: Root hairs: Tubular extensions of epidermal cells. Increase root surface area 10-20× for water and mineral absorption. Secrete acids and enzymes to dissolve soil minerals. Critical for plant water uptake.

classifications.type: Ground Tissue: The Plant's Factory and Warehouse
classifications.value: Understanding ground tissue explains how plants make food (photosynthesis), store it (starch, water), and support themselves (flexible and rigid support).
characteristics: Parenchyma: Thin-walled, living cells. Perform photosynthesis (chlorenchyma), storage (starch, water), secretion, and wound healing (totipotency). Most common plant cell.
Collenchyma: Unevenly thickened primary walls, living cells. Provide flexible support in growing stems, petioles, and veins. Stretch as plant grows.
Sclerenchyma: Thick secondary walls with lignin, dead at maturity. Provide rigid support in mature tissues. Two types: fibers (long, slender) and sclereids (irregular).
Cortex: Ground tissue between dermal and vascular tissue in roots and stems. Stores starch, provides support.
Pith: Ground tissue in center of stems. Stores water and nutrients.
Mesophyll: Ground tissue in leaves. Palisade mesophyll (columnar, dense chloroplasts) for light capture; spongy mesophyll (irregular, air spaces) for gas exchange.
classifications.behavior:
Ground tissue is the largest part of the plant. It does the work. In leaves, it is called mesophyll and is specialized for photosynthesis. In stems, it is called cortex and pith and is specialized for storage and support. In roots, it is called cortex and is specialized for storage and transport to the vascular tissue.

The three cell types of ground tissue have different jobs. Parenchyma cells are the workhorses. They have thin walls so that gases and nutrients can move in and out easily. They perform photosynthesis (in leaves), store starch (in roots and tubers), and secrete nectar and resins. They also have an amazing ability: if they are damaged, they can divide and turn into other cell types. This is called totipotency, and it is why you can grow a whole plant from a cutting.

Collenchyma cells provide flexible support. They have thick walls, but the thickening is uneven—it is thickest at the corners where cells meet. This gives the stem strength without making it rigid. Collenchyma is found in young stems and leaf stalks (petioles). It allows the plant to bend in the wind without breaking. You can see collenchyma as the "strings" in celery.

Sclerenchyma cells provide rigid support. They have thick secondary walls filled with lignin, which is hard and waterproof. They are dead at maturity—only the cell wall remains. This makes them very strong and durable. Wood is mostly sclerenchyma fibers. Sclereids (another type of sclerenchyma) make seed coats hard and pear fruit gritty.

The arrangement of ground tissue varies by organ. In leaves, the mesophyll is organized into palisade (for light capture) and spongy (for gas exchange). In stems, the cortex (outer) and pith (center) store water and starch. In roots, the cortex is thick and stores starch that the plant will use for growth.

examples.explanation:
The mesophyll is the ground tissue of the leaf. It is where photosynthesis happens. In most plants, the mesophyll is divided into two layers.

The palisade mesophyll is just under the upper epidermis. The cells are columnar (like pillars) and packed tightly together. They contain many chloroplasts (40-100 per cell) to capture as much light as possible. The palisade layer is where most of the photosynthesis occurs.

The spongy mesophyll is below the palisade layer. The cells are irregular and have large air spaces between them. These air spaces connect to the stomata, allowing carbon dioxide to diffuse into the leaf and oxygen to diffuse out. The spongy layer is where gas exchange happens.

This organization—dense chloroplasts above, air spaces below—is an adaptation for efficient photosynthesis. Light hits the top of the leaf first, so that is where the chloroplasts are concentrated. Air enters from the bottom (where stomata are usually located), so that is where the air spaces are. The two layers work together: palisade captures light, spongy exchanges gases.

image.alt: Leaf cross-section showing palisade mesophyll (columnar, dense chloroplasts) and spongy mesophyll (irregular, air spaces)
image.caption: Mesophyll: Palisade (columnar cells, many chloroplasts) captures light; Spongy (irregular cells, air spaces) allows gas exchange. Organization optimizes photosynthesis: light captured above, CO₂ enters from below.

examples.explanation:
Many plants store food in ground tissue. This allows them to survive through winter, drought, or other harsh conditions, and to regrow quickly when conditions improve.

Carrots, beets, and radishes are storage roots. Most of the root is cortex—ground tissue filled with storage parenchyma. These cells are large and thin-walled, and they are packed with starch or sugars. Carrots are orange because they also store pigments called carotenoids.

Potatoes are not roots; they are modified stems called tubers. The $eyes$ of a potato are buds. The rest of the potato is ground tissue (pith and cortex) filled with starch. A single potato plant can produce many tubers, each packed with enough energy to grow a new plant.

Onions are modified leaves. The fleshy layers are leaf bases filled with storage parenchyma. Onions store sugars and water, which is why they are crisp and sweet.

Cacti store water in their stems. The stem is thick and succulent, with large vacuoles in the parenchyma cells that can hold huge amounts of water. A saguaro cactus can store thousands of gallons of water in its stem, enough to survive years of drought.

image.alt: Storage organs: carrot (storage root, cortex), potato (tuber, pith and cortex), cactus (stem, water-storage parenchyma)
image.caption: Storage ground tissue: Carrots (root cortex stores sugar), Potatoes (stem pith/cortex stores starch), Cacti (stem parenchyma stores water). Storage allows plants to survive harsh conditions and regrow quickly.

classifications.value: Understanding vascular tissue explains how plants transport water from roots to leaves and sugar from leaves to the rest of the plant.
characteristics: Xylem: Transports water and minerals upward from roots to shoots. Made of dead cells (tracheids and vessel elements) with hollow interiors and lignified walls.
Phloem: Transports sugar (sucrose) from sources (leaves, storage organs) to sinks (growing roots, shoots, fruits). Made of living cells (sieve tubes with companion cells).
Tracheids: Long, tapered xylem cells with pits (openings) in their walls for water movement between cells.
Vessel elements: Shorter, wider xylem cells with perforated end plates, forming continuous tubes (vessels) for efficient water flow.
Sieve tubes: Phloem conducting cells arranged end-to-end, with sieve plates (porous walls) between them.
Companion cells: Phloem cells that support sieve tubes, providing ATP, proteins, and signaling molecules.
classifications.behavior:
The vascular system is the plant's plumbing. It has two types of pipes.

Xylem carries water and minerals from the roots up to the leaves. It is made of dead cells. Why dead? Because water is pulled up by tension (like sucking on a straw), and living cells would collapse under that tension. Dead cells with thick, lignified walls can handle the tension without collapsing. The water moves through xylem because of transpiration—water evaporating from the leaves creates a suction that pulls water up from the roots. This is called the cohesion-tension theory. Water molecules stick to each other (cohesion) and to the xylem walls (adhesion), so the water column does not break. If it does break (from air bubbles, called cavitation), the xylem cannot transport water until the bubble is dissolved.

Phloem carries sugar from where it is made (source) to where it is needed (sink). Sources are usually leaves (where photosynthesis happens) or storage organs (where starch is broken down). Sinks are growing roots, shoots, fruits, and seeds. Phloem is made of living cells. Sugar is pumped into the phloem at the source, which causes water to flow in by osmosis. This creates high pressure. At the sink, sugar is removed, water flows out, and pressure drops. The pressure difference pushes the sugar solution through the phloem. This is called the pressure-flow hypothesis. Phloem can move sugar in either direction—from leaves to roots in summer, from roots to shoots in spring.

Xylem and phloem are usually found together in vascular bundles. In stems, these bundles are arranged in a ring (in eudicots) or scattered (in monocots). In roots, they form a central cylinder (stele). In leaves, they form veins.

Wood is mostly secondary xylem produced by the vascular cambium (a layer of dividing cells between xylem and phloem). Each year, trees add a new layer of xylem, creating growth rings. Wide rings mean good growing conditions; narrow rings mean stress.

examples.process: Xylem: How Water Gets to the Top of a Tree
examples.explanation:
A 100-meter redwood tree has no heart to pump water to its top. How does water get up there? The answer is transpiration and cohesion.

Water evaporates from the leaves through the stomata (transpiration). As water molecules leave the leaf, they pull on the water molecules behind them. This tension is transmitted all the way down the xylem to the roots. It is like sucking on a long straw—the suction at the top pulls water up from the bottom.

Water molecules are sticky. They stick to each other (cohesion) and to the walls of the xylem (adhesion). This keeps the water column from breaking. Even under tension, the column stays intact.

The xylem itself is made of hollow tubes. In angiosperms (flowering plants), xylem has vessel elements—short, wide cells with open ends that stack to form long pipes. Water can flow quickly through these pipes. In gymnosperms (conifers), xylem has tracheids—long, tapered cells with pits (small openings) in their walls. Water moves more slowly through tracheids, but they are better at resisting air bubbles (cavitation).

If an air bubble enters the xylem, the column breaks, and that xylem vessel stops working. This is why trees need to be constantly growing new xylem. In dry conditions, cavitation can be a serious problem, which is why drought-stressed trees often die from the top down—the water column breaks in the highest xylem first.

image.alt: Xylem vessels showing water movement by transpiration pull: water evaporates from leaves, pulls water up through xylem
image.caption: Xylem transport: Water evaporates from leaves (transpiration) → creates tension → pulls water up through xylem. Cohesion (water sticks to water) and adhesion (water sticks to xylem walls) keep column intact.

examples.process: Phloem: How Sugar Gets from Leaves to Roots
examples.explanation:
While xylem moves water up, phloem moves sugar down (and sometimes up). Sugar is made in the leaves by photosynthesis. It needs to get to the roots, the growing tips, the flowers, and the fruits. Phloem does this job.

Phloem is made of living cells. Sieve tube elements are the conducting cells. They are connected end-to-end, with porous walls (sieve plates) between them. Their companion cells are the support cells—they keep the sieve tubes alive (since sieve tubes lose their nuclei and many organelles).

Here is how phloem works. At the source (a leaf), sugar is pumped into the sieve tubes. Water follows by osmosis, because the sugar makes the phloem solution more concentrated. This creates high pressure at the source.

At the sink (a growing root), sugar is removed from the sieve tubes. Water follows out, and pressure drops. The pressure difference pushes the sugar solution from the source to the sink.

This is called the pressure-flow hypothesis. It explains how phloem can move sugar in either direction. In summer, leaves are the main source, and roots are a sink—sugar moves down. In spring, before leaves emerge, roots are the source (starch broken down to sugar) and buds are the sink—sugar moves up.

image.alt: Phloem sieve tubes showing pressure flow: sugar loaded at source (high pressure), unloaded at sink (low pressure), flow from high to low pressure
image.caption: Phloem transport: Sugar loaded into sieve tubes at source → water follows → high pressure. Sugar unloaded at sink → water follows out → low pressure. Pressure difference pushes sap from source to sink.

sections.section_title: Fundamental Principles of Plant Tissue Systems

principles.statement: Roots, stems, and leaves have the same three tissue systems, but they are organized differently to match each organ's function.
principles.application:
Think about what each organ does. Roots absorb water and minerals from the soil. They need a large surface area (root hairs), a way to store nutrients (cortex), and a way to transport water up to the stem (central stele with xylem). They do not need to support themselves against gravity (soil does that) or capture light (they are underground). So roots have: dermal with root hairs, thick cortex for storage, and a central vascular cylinder.

Stems hold the plant up against gravity and transport water and sugar between roots and leaves. They need to be strong (sclerenchyma fibers) and flexible (collenchyma) while still growing. They also need to store water and nutrients. So stems have: epidermis (or periderm in woody plants), cortex and pith for storage, and vascular bundles arranged for support (ring in eudicots, scattered in monocots).

Leaves capture light for photosynthesis. They need to be thin so light can penetrate, have a large surface area to capture more light, and have stomata for gas exchange. They also need veins to bring water in and take sugar out. So leaves have: epidermis with stomata (usually on the underside), mesophyll for photosynthesis (palisade and spongy), and veins (vascular bundles).

This principle applies to all plants. By looking at a cross-section, you can usually tell whether it is a root, stem, or leaf just from the tissue arrangement.
principles.example:
Compare a root cross-section and a stem cross-section under a microscope. In the root, you see a central stele (vascular tissue in a cylinder) with xylem in a star pattern. Around it is the cortex (ground tissue for storage). The epidermis has root hairs (if you are looking at an older part, they may be gone).

In the stem, you see a ring of vascular bundles (in eudicots) or scattered bundles (in monocots). The ground tissue is divided into cortex (outside the ring) and pith (inside the ring). The epidermis is a single layer with a cuticle but no root hairs.

These differences are not random. They reflect what each organ does. The root is built for absorption and storage; the stem is built for transport and support.
principles.analogy: Think of a house. The foundation (root) is built differently from the walls (stem) and the roof (leaf). The foundation is underground, has thick concrete, and connects to the ground. The walls are vertical, support the roof, and have windows for light and air. The roof is thin, slanted, and covered with shingles to keep water out. All are part of the same house, but each is built differently for its job. Plants do the same thing with their tissue systems.

image.alt: Cross-sections of root, stem, and leaf showing different tissue arrangements that match organ function
image.caption: Tissue organization matches function: Root (absorption—root hairs, thick cortex, central stele), Stem (support/transport—vascular bundles, cortex, pith), Leaf (photosynthesis—mesophyll, veins, stomata). Same systems, different arrangements.

principles.principle: Tissue Systems Are Continuous Throughout the Plant
principles.statement: The three tissue systems are not separate in each organ—they are continuous from roots to stems to leaves, forming an integrated whole.
principles.application:
The dermal system is continuous from the root tips to the leaf tips. The epidermis of the root connects to the epidermis of the stem, which connects to the epidermis of the leaf. In woody plants, the periderm (bark) is continuous from the trunk to the branches.

The ground system is also continuous. The cortex of the root connects to the cortex of the stem, which connects to the mesophyll of the leaf through the petiole. The pith of the stem is continuous through the nodes and internodes.

The vascular system is the most obviously continuous. Xylem and phloem run from the roots, through the stem, into the leaves. In the roots, xylem and phloem are in the stele. In the stem, they are in vascular bundles that run the length of the stem. In the leaves, they are in veins. If you cut a stem, you break the vascular continuity, and the plant may wilt or die.

This continuity is why a plant can function as a single organism. Water absorbed by root hairs travels through xylem to the leaves. Sugar made in leaves travels through phloem to the roots. Signals (hormones) travel through the vascular system to coordinate growth and responses to the environment.
principles.example:
Girdling is a technique used to kill trees or to study phloem transport. If you remove a ring of bark (which contains the phloem) from around a tree trunk, the tree will die. Why? Because the xylem is still intact (it is deep in the wood), so water can still reach the leaves. But the phloem is gone, so sugar cannot travel from the leaves to the roots. The roots starve, stop taking up water, and the tree dies. This demonstrates vascular continuity—break it in one place, and the whole system fails.

The opposite experiment is to remove a ring of xylem (by drilling into the wood). The tree will die quickly because water cannot reach the leaves. Again, continuity is essential.
principles.analogy: Tissue continuity is like the circulatory system in an animal. The heart pumps blood through arteries, which branch into capillaries, which then return through veins. If you cut a major artery, the animal bleeds out. If you cut a major vein, blood pools. The plant's vascular system is similar—break it, and the plant dies. The dermal and ground systems are also continuous, like the skin and connective tissue that hold the animal together.

image.alt: Diagram showing dermal, ground, and vascular tissues continuous from roots through stems to leaves
image.caption: Tissue continuity: Dermal (outer layer continuous), Ground (cortex and pith continuous through nodes), Vascular (xylem and phloem continuous from roots to leaves). Break continuity in one place, whole plant suffers.

principles.principle: Primary and Secondary Growth Change Tissue Organization
principles.statement: Plants grow in two ways: primary growth (increase in length) from apical meristems, and secondary growth (increase in thickness) from lateral meristems. Secondary growth dramatically changes tissue organization in stems and roots.
principles.application:
All plants have primary growth. The apical meristems at the tips of roots and shoots produce new cells that differentiate into the three tissue systems. This is how roots get longer and stems get taller.

Some plants (woody eudicots and gymnosperms) also have secondary growth. Two lateral meristems are responsible. The vascular cambium is a layer of dividing cells between the xylem and phloem. It produces secondary xylem (wood) to the inside and secondary phloem to the outside. Each year, a new layer of xylem is added, creating growth rings. The cork cambium produces periderm (bark) to the outside, replacing the epidermis.

Secondary growth changes the tissue organization dramatically. In a young stem, the vascular bundles are separate, and the cortex and pith are large. In an older stem, the vascular cambium has produced so much secondary xylem that the original vascular bundles are crushed, and the pith is small. The cortex is replaced by periderm.

Monocots and herbaceous plants do not have secondary growth. Their stems stay herbaceous (soft) and do not increase in thickness over time.
principles.example:
Look at a tree trunk cross-section. You can see the growth rings (secondary xylem). The center (pith) is small. The vascular cambium is a thin layer just inside the bark. The bark (periderm) is the outer layer. The wood (secondary xylem) is the bulk of the trunk. This is very different from a young stem, where the pith is large, the vascular bundles are separate, and the cortex is prominent. Secondary growth has transformed the tissue organization.

In a palm tree (a monocot), there is no secondary growth. The trunk is made of primary tissues—vascular bundles scattered in ground tissue. The palm cannot increase in thickness over time, which is why palm trunks are the same width from bottom to top.
principles.analogy: Primary growth is like a building's frame going up; secondary growth is like adding insulation, wiring, and finishing. The frame (primary tissues) gives the basic shape. The finishing (secondary tissues) adds thickness, strength, and protection. Some buildings (herbaceous plants) stay as just the frame; others (trees) get thick walls, plumbing, and exterior cladding.

image.alt: Comparison of young stem (primary growth only) and tree trunk (secondary growth) showing changes in tissue organization
image.caption: Primary growth: Young stem with separate vascular bundles, large pith and cortex. Secondary growth: Tree trunk with annual rings (secondary xylem), small pith, bark (periderm). Vascular cambium produces new xylem each year.

sections.section_title: Key Components of Plant Tissue Systems

components.definition: Plant tissue systems are made of three components: dermal (epidermis, periderm), ground (parenchyma, collenchyma, sclerenchyma), and vascular (xylem, phloem). Their arrangement in each organ determines the organ's function.
components.interpretation: The plant's ability to survive depends on all three tissue systems working together. Dermal protects and regulates exchange; ground does the metabolic work and provides support; vascular transports resources. If any system fails, the plant suffers. Understanding these components helps us diagnose plant problems (wilting = vascular or dermal issue, yellowing = ground tissue issue) and improve crops.

image.alt: Integrated diagram of the three tissue systems showing how they work together
image.caption: Tissue systems integration: Dermal (protects, exchanges gases), Ground (photosynthesis, storage, support), Vascular (transports water and sugar). All three work together to keep the plant alive.

categories.type: Vascular Bundle Patterns: Monocots vs. Eudicots
categories.description: The arrangement of vascular bundles in stems is one of the main ways to tell monocots and eudicots apart. This reflects their different growth strategies.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
In eudicot stems (oak, sunflower, maple), the vascular bundles are arranged in a ring. Outside the ring is the cortex; inside the ring is the pith. Between the xylem and phloem in each bundle is the vascular cambium, which allows secondary growth. This arrangement is good for plants that grow thick over time—the ring can expand outward, and the pith can be compressed.

In monocot stems (corn, grass, palm), the vascular bundles are scattered throughout the ground tissue. There is no clear cortex or pith. There is no vascular cambium, so monocots cannot undergo secondary growth. This arrangement is good for plants that stay slender and flexible—the scattered bundles provide support in all directions, allowing the stem to bend without breaking.

In roots, both monocots and eudicots have a central stele (cylinder of vascular tissue), not bundles. The xylem is often star-shaped with phloem between the arms. Monocot roots may have a pith in the center of the stele; eudicot roots usually do not.

These patterns are so consistent that you can identify a plant as monocot or eudicot just by looking at a stem cross-section under a microscope.

examples.explanation:
A sunflower stem cross-section shows a ring of vascular bundles. Each bundle has xylem on the inside (toward the pith) and phloem on the outside (toward the cortex). Between the xylem and phloem is the vascular cambium—a thin layer of dividing cells.

In a young stem, the bundles are separate. As the stem ages, the vascular cambium produces secondary xylem to the inside and secondary phloem to the outside. The bundles grow outward and eventually merge, forming a continuous cylinder of xylem and phloem. The cortex and pith may be crushed or remain as storage tissue.

This arrangement is ideal for plants that grow tall and thick. The ring of bundles provides structural support, and the vascular cambium allows the stem to increase in diameter year after year. That is why oak trees can become massive—they keep adding wood each year.

image.alt: Eudicot stem cross-section showing ring of vascular bundles with cortex outside and pith inside
image.caption: Eudicot stem: Vascular bundles arranged in ring. Xylem (inside), phloem (outside), vascular cambium between. Allows secondary growth—trees can increase diameter each year.

examples.explanation:
A corn stem cross-section shows vascular bundles scattered throughout the ground tissue. There is no clear cortex or pith—the bundles are everywhere. Each bundle has xylem and phloem, but there is no vascular cambium.

Because there is no vascular cambium, monocot stems cannot increase in diameter. They grow only in length (primary growth). This is why grass stems are the same width from bottom to top, and palm trunks do not get thicker over time.

The scattered arrangement provides support in all directions. A grass stem can bend in the wind without breaking because the bundles are distributed throughout the stem, not just in a ring. This is an adaptation for plants that are exposed to wind and need flexibility.

image.alt: Monocot stem cross-section showing vascular bundles scattered throughout ground tissue
image.caption: Monocot stem: Vascular bundles scattered in ground tissue. No cortex/pith distinction. No vascular cambium—no secondary growth. Scattered bundles provide support in all directions, allowing flexibility.

categories.description: Plants from different environments have modified their tissue systems to survive. These modifications are visible under the microscope.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Plants in dry environments (xerophytes) have adaptations to reduce water loss. Their cuticle is thick (sometimes multiple layers). Their stomata are often sunken in pits, sheltered from wind. Their leaves may be reduced (cactus spines) or have dense trichomes that trap moisture. The ground tissue may have water-storage cells (succulence). The vascular tissue may have smaller xylem vessels that resist cavitation (air bubbles) in dry conditions.

Plants in wet environments (hydrophytes) have adaptations to deal with excess water and low oxygen. Their cuticle is thin or absent. Their stomata are often on the upper surface (not underwater). Their ground tissue has aerenchyma—large air spaces that provide buoyancy and allow oxygen to reach submerged roots. Their vascular tissue may be reduced.

Plants in shady environments (shade plants) have adaptations to capture light. Their leaves are thin and large, with a single layer of palisade mesophyll (instead of multiple layers). Their ground tissue has fewer chloroplasts (they do not need as many in low light). Their stems are elongated (to reach light).

Plants in windy environments have adaptations to resist breaking. Their collenchyma and sclerenchyma are thicker (thigmomorphogenesis). Their stems are shorter and thicker. Their vascular bundles may be more numerous.

examples.process: Aerenchyma: Air Spaces in Aquatic Plants
examples.explanation:
Aerenchyma is a type of ground tissue with large air spaces. It is common in aquatic plants (water lilies, rushes) and in plants that grow in waterlogged soil (rice).

In a water lily, the leaf stem (petiole) has large air channels running its length. These channels are aerenchyma. They do two things. First, they make the leaf buoyant, so it floats on the water surface. Second, they allow oxygen to diffuse from the leaves down to the roots. The roots are in mud that has almost no oxygen; without aerenchyma, they would suffocate.

In rice, aerenchyma forms in the roots when the soil is flooded. The plant senses low oxygen and triggers programmed cell death in the root cortex, creating air spaces. This allows oxygen from the leaves to reach the roots. Rice can grow in flooded paddies because of this adaptation.

Aerenchyma shows how ground tissue can be modified for a specific environment. In most plants, ground tissue is solid. In aquatic plants, it is full of air.

image.alt: Water lily petiole cross-section showing large air spaces (aerenchyma), and rice root showing aerenchyma in cortex
image.caption: Aerenchyma: Ground tissue with large air spaces. Water lily petiole (buoyancy, oxygen transport), rice root (oxygen to roots in flooded soil). Adaptation to aquatic and waterlogged environments.

sections.section_title: Worked Example: Identifying Plant Organs from Tissue Organization

worked_examples.title: Classifying Plant Organs from Cross-Sections
worked_examples.problem: You are looking at three plant cross-sections under a microscope. Section A: Has a central stele with xylem in a star pattern, a thick cortex, and an epidermis with root hairs. Section B: Has a ring of vascular bundles, a cortex outside the ring, a pith inside the ring, and an epidermis with a cuticle. Section C: Has a single layer of palisade cells, spongy cells with air spaces below, veins scattered through the tissue, and an epidermis with stomata. Identify each organ (root, stem, leaf) and explain how you know.

step_by_step.calculation: Central stele with star-shaped xylem + thick cortex + root hairs → Root. Root hairs are diagnostic—only roots have them. The central stele (vascular cylinder) is typical of roots, not stems or leaves. The thick cortex stores starch. This is a root cross-section, likely from the absorption zone where root hairs are present.

Ring of vascular bundles + cortex + pith + cuticle → Stem. The ring arrangement of vascular bundles is typical of eudicot stems. Cortex and pith are ground tissue regions. The cuticle on the epidermis is typical of aerial organs (stems and leaves), not roots. This is a young stem cross-section.

Palisade layer + spongy layer + veins + stomata → Leaf. Palisade and spongy mesophyll are unique to leaves. The veins (vascular bundles) run through the mesophyll. Stomata are present in the epidermis (usually on the underside). This is a leaf cross-section, likely from a eudicot (palisade and spongy layers distinct).

Root: central stele, thick cortex, root hairs. Stem: vascular bundles in ring (or scattered), cortex and pith, cuticle. Leaf: palisade and spongy mesophyll, veins, stomata. Each organ has a unique tissue organization that reflects its function—absorption (root), support/transport (stem), photosynthesis (leaf).

worked_examples.final_answer: (1) A: Root; B: Stem; C: Leaf. (2) Features: A (central stele, root hairs), B (ring of bundles, cortex, pith), C (palisade/spongy mesophyll, stomata). (3) Function: Root absorbs, stem supports and transports, leaf photosynthesizes. (4) Tissue organization matches organ function. This demonstrates how to identify plant organs from microscopic cross-sections.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Plant organs are identified by their tissue organization. Roots have a central stele, thick cortex, and root hairs. Stems have vascular bundles in a ring (eudicot) or scattered (monocot), with cortex and pith. Leaves have palisade and spongy mesophyll, veins, and stomata. These patterns are consistent across plants and reflect each organ's function.

sections.introduction: Plant tissue systems develop from meristems through a process of cell division, expansion, and differentiation. Understanding this flow reveals how plants build functional tissues.

process_flow.description: Apical meristems produce new cells that will become the three tissue systems.
process_flow.mechanism: Shoot and root tips have apical meristems—regions of actively dividing cells. As cells divide, some remain in the meristem (to keep it going), while others are displaced into the elongation zone. These displaced cells are still undifferentiated. They will become protoderm (dermal), ground meristem (ground), or procambium (vascular) based on their position and signals from neighboring cells.
process_flow.result: Three primary meristems established: protoderm, ground meristem, procambium.

image.alt: Apical meristem showing protoderm (outer), ground meristem (middle), and procambium (inner) regions
image.caption: Apical meristem: Cells divide → differentiate into protoderm (dermal), ground meristem (ground), procambium (vascular). Position determines fate. Primary meristems give rise to tissue systems.

process_flow.description: Cells expand and differentiate into specific cell types within each tissue system.
process_flow.mechanism: Cells take up water, vacuoles expand, and cells enlarge. Protoderm cells flatten, secrete cuticle, and may form stomata, trichomes, or root hairs. Ground meristem cells become parenchyma (thin-walled), collenchyma (uneven thickening), or sclerenchyma (secondary walls, lignin). Procambium cells become xylem (tracheids, vessels) and phloem (sieve tubes, companion cells). Differentiation is controlled by hormones and transcription factors.
process_flow.result: Specialized cells mature, forming functional tissues.

image.alt: Differentiation of primary meristems into specialized cell types in each tissue system
image.caption: Differentiation: Protoderm → epidermis (cuticle, stomata, trichomes, root hairs). Ground meristem → parenchyma, collenchyma, sclerenchyma. Procambium → xylem, phloem. Specialized cells form functional tissues.

process_flow.description: Tissues integrate into functional organs, with vascular connections linking all parts.
process_flow.mechanism: Dermal tissue forms continuous outer layer. Ground tissue fills interior, with cortex and pith in stems, cortex in roots, mesophyll in leaves. Vascular tissue forms bundles (stems) or stele (roots) that connect to leaves via vascular traces. Plasmodesmata connect cells within tissues; xylem and phloem connect organs. Integration enables water transport from roots to leaves, sugar transport from leaves to roots, and signal transmission throughout plant.
process_flow.result: Functional organs (roots, stems, leaves) with integrated tissue systems enable whole-plant function.

image.alt: Root, stem, and leaf showing integrated tissue systems and vascular connections
image.caption: Tissue integration: Dermal (continuous outer layer), Ground (connected through nodes), Vascular (continuous from roots to leaves). Enables water up, sugar down, signals throughout. Whole-plant function from integrated tissues.

sections.section_title: Advanced Concepts: Tissue Systems in Agriculture and Biotechnology
sections.definition: Understanding plant tissue systems allows us to manipulate plants for agriculture, forestry, and biotechnology. We can breed plants with improved tissues, use tissue culture to clone plants, and engineer vascular systems for better transport.

process_types.type: Tissue Culture: Growing Plants from Tissues
process_types.energy_profile: Dedifferentiation requires energy for cell wall remodeling and cell division; regeneration requires sustained metabolism.
process_types.direction: Differentiated tissue → dedifferentiation → callus → organogenesis → whole plant
process_types.function: Clone plants, produce disease-free plants, conserve endangered species, and create genetically modified plants.
process_types.energy_source: ATP from respiration fuels cell wall remodeling, DNA replication, and cell division during regeneration.

primary_example.explanation:
Bananas are propagated vegetatively because they are sterile (seedless). For centuries, farmers used suckers (shoots from the base of the plant) to grow new banana plants. But suckers can carry diseases. Tissue culture provides a way to grow disease-free banana plants.

Steps: (1) A small piece of meristem tissue is removed from a healthy banana plant. (2) The tissue is placed on sterile nutrient medium with sugar, vitamins, and hormones. (3) The tissue forms callus (undifferentiated cells). (4) The callus is transferred to medium with hormones that promote shoot formation. (5) Shoots are transferred to medium with hormones that promote root formation. (6) Plantlets are moved to soil.

From a single meristem, thousands of identical, disease-free banana plants can be produced. This is called micropropagation. It is used for many crops: orchids, potatoes, strawberries, and trees.

This technology relies on the totipotency of ground tissue (parenchyma) to dedifferentiate and redifferentiate into whole plants. Animal cells cannot do this—plant cells are unique in their regenerative ability.

image.alt: Tissue culture steps: explant → callus → shoots → roots → plantlet
image.caption: Tissue culture: Explant (meristem) → callus (dedifferentiation) → shoots (high cytokinin) → roots (high auxin) → plantlet. Thousands of identical, disease-free plants from a single meristem. Relies on ground tissue totipotency.

image.alt: Applications of tissue culture: micropropagation, genetic engineering, conservation
image.caption: Tissue culture applications: Micropropagation (cloning bananas, orchids), Genetic engineering (transgenic crops), Conservation (endangered species). Ground tissue parenchyma totipotency makes these possible.

process_types.energy_profile: Modifying tissue systems requires genetic or breeding investment but can improve yield, stress tolerance, and quality.
process_types.direction: Wild-type tissue → genetic modification/breeding → altered tissue structure → improved traits
process_types.function: Improve crop productivity, stress resilience, and quality through tissue modification.
goals: Strengthen stems to resist lodging (falling over)
Improve water transport for drought tolerance
Increase storage capacity for higher yields
Modify fruit texture for better eating quality
process_types.energy_source: Genetic modification requires research investment; breeding requires selection over generations; both produce plants with improved tissue systems.

primary_example.explanation:
Lodging is when crop stems fall over (bend or break). This is a major problem in wheat, rice, and other grains. Lodged plants produce less grain and are harder to harvest.

Lodging resistance comes from stronger stems. Breeders select for wheat varieties with thicker sclerenchyma fibers in the stem, more vascular bundles, and stronger node connections. These traits make the stem stiffer and more resistant to bending.

Dwarf wheat varieties (developed by Norman Borlaug during the Green Revolution) have shorter, thicker stems that resist lodging. This allowed farmers to use more fertilizer and irrigation without the wheat falling over. The result was a dramatic increase in wheat yields.

This is an example of modifying ground tissue (sclerenchyma) and stem architecture to improve crop productivity. Understanding plant tissue systems allows breeders to select for better structural traits.

image.alt: Comparison of lodged wheat (fallen over) and standing wheat (lodging-resistant), with cross-section showing thicker sclerenchyma in resistant variety
image.caption: Lodging resistance: Wheat stems with thicker sclerenchyma fibers and stronger vascular bundles resist bending. Dwarf wheat varieties (Green Revolution) have shorter, thicker stems, dramatically increasing yields.

image.alt: Crop improvement through tissue modification: stronger stems, better water transport, increased storage, improved fruit texture
image.caption: Tissue engineering for crops: Stronger stems (lodging resistance), Better water transport (drought tolerance), Increased storage (higher yields), Improved fruit texture (quality). Understanding tissue systems enables targeted improvement.

sections.relationship: Tissue culture and crop improvement represent two ways we use our understanding of plant tissue systems. Tissue culture exploits totipotency for propagation and genetic engineering. Crop improvement modifies tissue structure for better yield, stress tolerance, and quality. Both rely on knowledge of how plant tissues are built and how they function.

sections.purpose: These are the essential vocabulary terms for understanding plant tissue systems. Knowing these terms will help you describe how plants are built and how they function.

activation.definition: The terms below cover the most important concepts in plant tissue biology. Each term is defined simply, with context to help you remember its significance.
activation.effect: Mastering these terms will allow you to discuss plant structure, function, and adaptation with confidence.

image.alt: Labeled diagrams of root, stem, and leaf cross-sections with key terms: epidermis, cortex, pith, vascular bundle, xylem, phloem, mesophyll, stoma
image.caption: Key plant tissue terms: Knowing these structures helps you identify plant organs and understand their functions.

key_properties.explanation: The outer protective layer of the plant. In young plants, it is the epidermis (single layer) with a waxy cuticle. In woody plants, it is replaced by periderm (bark). Includes stomata (gas exchange), trichomes (hairs), and root hairs (absorption).

The bulk of the plant, performing metabolism, support, and storage. Made of three cell types: parenchyma (thin walls, metabolism, storage), collenchyma (uneven walls, flexible support), and sclerenchyma (thick lignified walls, rigid support).

The transport system of the plant. Xylem carries water and minerals up from roots; phloem carries sugar from leaves to the rest of the plant. Xylem cells are dead at maturity; phloem cells are living.

Conducts water and dissolved minerals from roots to shoots. Made of dead cells (tracheids and vessel elements) with hollow interiors and lignified walls. Water moves by transpiration pull (cohesion-tension).

Conducts sugar (sucrose) from sources (leaves, storage) to sinks (roots, shoots, fruits). Made of living sieve tubes with companion cells. Sugar moves by pressure flow (high pressure at source, low pressure at sink).

The outermost layer of cells in young plants and non-woody parts. Secretes cuticle (waxy layer) to prevent water loss. Has stomata for gas exchange, trichomes for protection, and root hairs for absorption.
Pores in the epidermis, each flanked by two guard cells. Open to take in CO₂ for photosynthesis and release O₂; close to prevent water loss. Regulated by light, CO₂, and water availability.
Waxy layer secreted by the epidermis. Prevents water loss from the plant surface. Thicker in dry environments, thinner in wet environments.
Ground tissue between the epidermis and vascular tissue in roots and stems. In roots, it stores starch. In stems, it provides storage and support.
Ground tissue in the center of stems. Stores water and nutrients. May be large in young stems, reduced in woody stems.
Ground tissue in leaves. Palisade mesophyll (columnar cells with many chloroplasts) captures light; spongy mesophyll (irregular cells with air spaces) allows gas exchange.
A strand of vascular tissue containing xylem and phloem. In eudicot stems, arranged in a ring; in monocot stems, scattered; in leaves, form veins.
A layer of dividing cells between xylem and phloem in woody plants. Produces secondary xylem (wood) to the inside and secondary phloem to the outside, increasing stem thickness.
Bark that replaces epidermis in woody stems and roots. Made of cork cells (dead, waterproof) with lenticels for gas exchange. Protects against fire, insects, and temperature extremes.
Growth in length from apical meristems. All plants have primary growth. Produces primary tissues (epidermis, ground tissue, primary vascular tissue).

Growth in thickness from lateral meristems (vascular cambium, cork cambium). Occurs in woody eudicots and gymnosperms, not in monocots or herbaceous plants. Produces wood (secondary xylem) and bark (periderm).

A type of flowering plant with one seed leaf. Has scattered vascular bundles in stems, no secondary growth, parallel leaf veins, and flower parts in multiples of three. Examples: grasses, corn, orchids, palms.

A type of flowering plant with two seed leaves. Has vascular bundles in a ring in stems, secondary growth (in woody species), netted leaf veins, and flower parts in multiples of four or five. Examples: oaks, maples, sunflowers, beans.

The ability of a differentiated plant cell (especially parenchyma) to dedifferentiate, divide, and regenerate a whole plant. Unique to plants. Basis for plant tissue culture and vegetative propagation.
Ground tissue with large air spaces. Found in aquatic plants (water lilies) and plants in waterlogged soil (rice). Provides buoyancy and allows oxygen to reach submerged roots.

summary:
Plants have three tissue systems that span the entire plant: dermal (protection), ground (metabolism, support, storage), and vascular (transport). These systems are organized differently in roots, stems, and leaves to match each organ's function.

Dermal tissue is the outer layer. In young plants, it is the epidermis with a waxy cuticle (prevents water loss), stomata (gas exchange), trichomes (hairs), and root hairs (absorption). In woody plants, it is replaced by periderm (bark).

Ground tissue makes up most of the plant body. It has three cell types: parenchyma (thin walls, metabolism, storage, totipotency), collenchyma (uneven walls, flexible support in growing stems), and sclerenchyma (thick lignified walls, rigid support in mature parts). In leaves, ground tissue is mesophyll (palisade for light capture, spongy for gas exchange). In stems, it is cortex and pith (storage). In roots, it is cortex (storage).

Vascular tissue is the transport system. Xylem carries water and minerals upward from roots to leaves. It is made of dead cells (tracheids, vessel elements) and moves water by transpiration pull (cohesion-tension). Phloem carries sugar from leaves to the rest of the plant. It is made of living cells (sieve tubes with companion cells) and moves sugar by pressure flow.

Tissue organization varies by plant type. Eudicots have vascular bundles in a ring in stems, allowing secondary growth (trees). Monocots have scattered vascular bundles and no secondary growth (grasses, palms). Roots of both have a central stele.

Plants adapt their tissue systems to their environment. Dry-adapted plants have thick cuticles, sunken stomata, and water-storage parenchyma. Aquatic plants have aerenchyma (air spaces) for buoyancy and oxygen transport. Wind-exposed plants have thicker collenchyma and sclerenchyma.

Understanding plant tissue systems helps us grow better crops. We can breed plants with stronger stems (lodging resistance), better water transport (drought tolerance), and more efficient photosynthesis. We can use tissue culture to clone disease-free plants. From the food we eat to the wood we build with, plant tissues are the foundation of agriculture and forestry.
